title: Create and remove nodes

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Create and remove nodes; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: DOM creation builds elements, sets safe properties and text, then inserts fragments or nodes while preserving document semantics.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A list item is created with document.createElement, receives textContent, and is appended to a fragment. Your goal is to render a data collection with safe node creation and stable semantic structure. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scope, closures and modules; html project site; Query and update DOM; Array transformation methods. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: DOM creation builds elements, sets safe properties and text, then inserts fragments or nodes while preserving document semantics. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Repeated insertion can move existing nodes, custom elements have lifecycle behavior, and large updates can cost layout work. This draft therefore rejects: Appending the same node twice creates two independent copies.

includes: Build document fragments and preserve semantics, ownership and focus during updates.
Render a data collection with safe node creation and stable semantic structure.
excludes: Repeated insertion can move existing nodes, custom elements have lifecycle behavior, and large updates can cost layout work.

outcomes: Render a data collection with safe node creation and stable semantic structure.

examples.title: Create and remove nodes: concrete case
examples.situation: A list item is created with document.createElement, receives textContent, and is appended to a fragment.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A list item is created with document.createElement, receives textContent, and is appended to a fragment.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Inserting an existing node moves that node; clone explicitly when a separate copy is required.
examples.result: The result follows because Inserting an existing node moves that node; clone explicitly when a separate copy is required.
